questions.question: Which set lists the major criteria used in this section to classify DBMSs?
options: Data model, user support, distribution, cost and purpose
CPU speed, RAM size, monitor type, keyboard and mouse
Only table size and row count
Only programming language and operating system
Backup date, password length and screen resolution
questions.explanation: DBMSs can be grouped by how data is modeled, how many users are supported, where data is distributed, licensing cost and intended purpose.

questions.question: Which type of DBMS stores data primarily in tables containing rows and columns?
questions.explanation: An RDBMS represents data using relations or tables organized into rows and columns.

questions.question: Which combination is most closely associated with an RDBMS?
options: Tree nodes with one parent only
SQL, keys, normalization and ACID transactions
Records connected only by physical pointers
questions.explanation: Relational systems commonly use SQL, primary and foreign keys, normalization and ACID transaction properties.

questions.question: An Object-Oriented DBMS is most suitable when an application must store:
questions.explanation: OODBMSs naturally store objects and support object-oriented concepts, making them useful for complex nested structures.

questions.question: Which is an example of an Object-Oriented DBMS?
questions.explanation: db4o is a commonly cited object-oriented database example.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes a hierarchical DBMS?
options: It organizes records as a tree in which a child normally has one parent
It requires every record to have multiple parents
It stores data only as key-value pairs
It is always a distributed NoSQL system
questions.explanation: The hierarchical model uses a tree structure with one-to-many parent-child relationships and normally one parent per child.

questions.question: Which product is the classic example of a hierarchical DBMS?
questions.explanation: IBM Information Management System (IMS) is a well-known hierarchical database system.

questions.question: What distinguishes a network DBMS from a hierarchical DBMS?
options: It allows only one child in the database
A record may participate in multiple parent-child links
questions.explanation: The network model supports web-like structures and multiple parents, making many-to-many relationships possible.

questions.question: Which is an example of a traditional network DBMS?
questions.explanation: IDMS is a classic example of the traditional network database model.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes a traditional network DBMS from a modern NoSQL graph database?
options: They are exactly the same model
A network DBMS stores only rows and columns
A graph database cannot represent relationships
A network DBMS is always newer than NoSQL
Network DBMS uses older record-pointer structures; graph DBMS is optimized for nodes and relationships
questions.explanation: Both may look graph-like, but the network model is an older record-and-pointer model, while graph databases such as Neo4j focus on nodes and relationships.

questions.question: The term NoSQL is commonly expanded as:
questions.explanation: NoSQL commonly means Not Only SQL and covers flexible non-relational models designed for large or varied data.

questions.question: Which pairing correctly identifies a document-oriented database?
questions.explanation: MongoDB stores flexible JSON-like documents and is a document-oriented NoSQL database.

questions.question: Which NoSQL product is primarily classified as a key-value store?
questions.explanation: Redis stores and retrieves data using keys mapped to values and is widely used for caching and sessions.

questions.question: Which NoSQL database is associated with the column-family model?
questions.explanation: Cassandra is a distributed column-family database often used for large-scale data and analytics workloads.

questions.question: Which database is designed for relationship-heavy data using nodes and edges?
questions.explanation: Neo4j is a graph database optimized for connected data such as social networks, recommendations and routes.

questions.question: Which pairing correctly represents a single-user DBMS example?
options: Oracle—only one user at a time
questions.explanation: SQLite is commonly used in personal and embedded applications and is cited as a single-user DBMS example in this classification.

questions.question: A bank database used simultaneously by thousands of customers is classified as:
questions.explanation: A multi-user DBMS permits many users to access and work with the database concurrently.

questions.question: A DBMS in which all data is maintained at one main location is classified as:
questions.explanation: A centralized DBMS stores and manages data at one central site or server.

questions.question: Which statement best describes a distributed DBMS?
options: It stores all data on one personal computer
Data is stored at multiple connected locations that appear as one database
It must use different DBMS software at every site
questions.explanation: A DDBMS coordinates data at multiple sites and presents the distributed collection as a unified database.

questions.question: A distributed system in which every branch uses MySQL is:
questions.explanation: A homogeneous DDBMS uses the same DBMS software and generally compatible models at all sites.

questions.question: Delhi uses Oracle, Mumbai uses MySQL and Patna uses SQL Server, with middleware connecting them. This is:
questions.explanation: Different DBMS products at different sites, integrated through middleware, form a heterogeneous DDBMS.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes an open-source DBMS?
options: It is always paid and its source is unavailable
Its source code is available and it commonly has community support
It can never be used by businesses
questions.explanation: Open-source DBMS software makes source code available and is commonly free to use, with community support and flexible licensing.

questions.question: Which product is the clearest example of a commercial DBMS in this section?
questions.explanation: Oracle Database is a standard example of a paid commercial DBMS offering professional support and enterprise features.

questions.question: A DBMS used for banking, hospitals, schools and ERP systems is classified by purpose as:
questions.explanation: A general-purpose DBMS supports many kinds of applications rather than one narrowly optimized task.

questions.question: A database optimized only for real-time telecom call recording is best classified as:
options: A relational DBMS by user support
questions.explanation: A special-purpose DBMS is designed and optimized for one specific task or application domain.

questions.question: The distinction between single-user and multi-user DBMS is based on:
options: The way records are physically linked
questions.explanation: User-support classification asks how many users can access the database—one or many.

questions.question: The distinction between centralized and distributed DBMS is based primarily on:
options: How many columns a table contains
questions.explanation: Distribution classification focuses on whether data is located at one site or coordinated across multiple sites.

questions.question: The open-source versus commercial distinction classifies DBMSs by:
questions.explanation: Open-source and commercial categories distinguish software by licensing, source availability, cost and support model.

questions.question: The distinction between general-purpose and special-purpose DBMS is based on:
options: Whether a tree model is used
Whether the source code is public
The number of concurrent transactions only
questions.explanation: Purpose classification asks whether the system supports many application types or is optimized for one specialized task.

questions.question: Which set of model-to-example mappings is entirely correct?
questions.explanation: MySQL is relational, MongoDB document-oriented, Redis key-value, Cassandra column-family and Neo4j graph-oriented.
